mnemonics.title: Critical Section Requirements
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER THE THREE REQUIREMENTS AS 'MPB': MUTUAL EXCLUSION (M), PROGRESS (P), BOUNDED WAITING (B). THINK 'MY PROGRESS BOUNDS' - MY process progression is bounded by these three rules.

mnemonics.title: Peterson's Solution Variables
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER PETERSON'S ALGORITHM USES 'FT': FLAG (F) AND TURN (T). BOTH PROCESSES CHECK 'FLAG' FOR DESIRE AND 'TURN' FOR COURTESY.

mnemonics.title: Test-and-Set Purpose
mnemonics.content: TEST-AND-SET = 'READ OLD + SET NEW' IN ONE ATOMIC STEP. REMEMBER IT AS 'ATOMIC SNATCH AND GRAB' - SNATCH THE OLD VALUE AND GRAB THE LOCK.

acronyms.expansion: Critical Section - the portion of code where shared resources are accessed
acronyms.usage: Used when discussing process synchronization and mutual exclusion problems

acronyms.expansion: Test-and-Set - a hardware atomic instruction that reads and modifies a variable in one operation
acronyms.usage: Used to implement atomic lock acquisition in multi-processor systems

acronyms.expansion: Race Condition - when timing of execution affects the final outcome of operations
acronyms.usage: The problem that critical section solutions aim to prevent

acronyms.expansion: Bounded Waiting - a requirement ensuring limited wait time for processes
acronyms.usage: One of the three essential requirements for critical section solutions

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two doors (processes) with a sign board (turn variable) and indicators (flag array). Only one process can go through at a time. The process checks: 'Do I want to enter?' (flag=true) and 'Is it my turn?' (turn=myID). If both true and other doesn't have stronger claim, I enter.

Think of a boolean as a locked door. Test-and-Set is like someone looking at the door (reading old value), finding it unlocked, and immediately locking it (setting to true) - all in one glance, so no one else can intervene between looking and locking.

Draw a timeline showing multiple processes. When one enters CS (colored block), others must wait. The entry section is like a gatekeeper checking permissions. Exit section is like the process putting up a 'vacancy' sign.

analogies.analogy: Think of a single-occupancy restroom in a restaurant. Only one person can use it at a time. The 'entry section' is knocking and checking if occupied. The 'critical section' is actually using the restroom. The 'exit section' is unlocking and leaving, making it available for others.

Two people trying to update the same Google Doc simultaneously without locking. Whichever saves last overwrites the other's changes. The final document depends on who clicked save last - creating inconsistent results based on timing.

In a queue at a ticket counter with a rule that no one can jump ahead of more than 3 people who were waiting before them. Even if someone arrives later, they cannot infinitely cut in front of early waiters. This ensures fairness.

Imagine a hotel room key system where checking out and getting a new key is ONE atomic action - you hand in your old key and receive a new one in the same instant, and no one else can get that room's key during that moment.
